MA388 Sabermetrics: Lesson 35

Shiny Apps

Author

LTC Jim Pleuss

Purpose

Last class we focused on generating slides using Quarto, and we explored ways to scale up your production to generate a set of slides for any combination of teams, years, etc.

What are some benefits of this capability?

  • Easily generate products at scale using a proven analysis pipeline.

  • You can email your generated product. There’s no web hosting.

What are some limitations?

  • If your product doesn’t answer the reader’s question, the reader has no way to refine the product to answer their question of interest.

  • The reader has very limited opportunity to interact with the data. The data doesn’t automatically travel with the static document you’ve created (possibly good or bad).

Shiny Apps!

A Shiny app is fundamentally different. Now you’ll host a small (though it could be quite large) application on a web server, and people will visit it via a published URL. It’s pretty simple:

  • Allow the user to select some permissible values of different parameters you’ll use to control any analysis you present.

  • Lay out a user interface (UI) and define server functions that turn user selections into tables, plots, etc.

Starting Example

Step 1: File -> New File -> Shiny Web App

Give the app a name with no spaces. I called mine “BattingLeaders” and selected the “Single File” option (default) and specified a directory where I wanted the app to live. I now have a folder in this locaiton called “BattingLeaders” with a single file titled “app.R” inside.

Step 2: Press the “Run App” button near the top right of the app editor. Drag the slider to different values and see what happens.

All the basics are here - a title, an input widget, and a reactive plot that changes as you change the input slider. This is basically it for Shiny Apps – you mix static content, user inputs, and reactive content.

Step 3: Explore the code that produced this app.

You should see a section called the ui that has elements you can connect to what you see in the app - a layout, some panels, a slider input, and a plot output.

Now look at the server section. There’s a single output element named distplot that is created in the server and made visible in the ui section as an argument to the plotOutput function. Notice that the plot code contained in this element would work anywhere with one exception – it is reliant on knowing what input$bins equals. You can guess where it gets that value – it’s from the sliderInput object named “bins” (the first element of an input object definition is its name/ID).

Step 4: Tweak this app to do something else.

  • Add static text.

After the titlePanel line in the ui, add this as a new line (including the comma):

h6("This is some really helpful explanation of what you're looking at."),

Run the app again to see what happened.

  • Add an input.

Put a comma at the end of the sliderInput definition after value=30). On the next line, add this text object:

h6("I couldn't think of anything good to add here, so I'm switching data sets:"), (include the comma)

…followed by this new input object:

radioButtons("cylinders", 
             label = h3("Select a number of cylinders to explore in mtcars:"),
             choices = list("4 cylinders" = 4, 
                            "6 cylinders" = 6, 
                            "8 cylinders" = 8), 
             selected = 4)

How do you learn what input widgets are available and how to employ them? Visit the Shiny Widget Gallery.

Run the app again to see what happened.

  • Add an output.

Let’s add a plot to the app that does something with mtcars and your input selection. Copy and paste the output$distPlot code block immediately underneath the existing code block (no need for a comma between the two plot blocks). Call this second plot block output$distPlot2 and add a second plotOutput object to the ui section like this:

mainPanel(
  plotOutput("distPlot"),
  plotOutput("distPlot2"),
)

Run the app again to see what happened. We should see our cylinders radio buttons and a second inter-eruption distribution plot. It’s time to connect these objects. First, add library(tidyverse) to the top of the app by the library(shiny) call. Next, change distPlot2 as follows:

output$distPlot2 <- renderPlot({
  mtcars |> 
    filter(cyl == input$cylinders) |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = mpg)) + 
    geom_histogram(bins = 6) + 
    labs(x = 'MPG',
         title = paste('Histogram of MPG for vehicles with', 
                       input$cylinders, 
                       "cylinders"))
})

That’s it! This basic knowledge – plus tons of creativity and trial and error – is what you need to make a much cooler, much more complicated app.

Challenge Problem

Make a Shiny App that allows the user to select a starting year and ending year between 1970 and 2023 as well as a choice of batting stats (start with batting average and home run total).

Display a histogram of all players’ stats during the time period of interest, showing only the histogram of the selected batting stat.
